ocr.text: The tables present data on various studies and metrics related to schizophrenia and immune response. In Table 1, the headers
include Geometric Mean (95% CI) and GMR Q-Pan/D-pan under the data range across groups, with values for HI titers pre-
purification showing a range of 80–3620, with a geometric mean of 929 (677–1273) for D-Pan and 814 (590–1122) for Q-Pan,
yielding a ratio of 0.88 (0.56–1.37). For HI titers purified IgG fraction, the range is 57–1280, with a mean of 453 (338–607) for
D-Pan and 413 (306–556) for Q-Pan, giving a ratio of 0.91 (0.60–1.39). Association rates (RU) range from 7.7–34.5, with
means of 15.4 (13.4–17.7) for D-Pan and 12.4 (10.8–14.3) for Q-Pan, resulting in a ratio of 0.81 (0.64–1.01). Dissociation rates
(%) range from 79.2–110.5, with means of 94.5 (92.5–96.5) for D-Pan and 95.5 (93.5–97.6) for Q-Pan, and a ratio of 1.01
(0.98–1.05). The kd (s−1) is reported as 1.1 × 10−8–1.7 × 10−4. Table 2 includes studies with headers for Study, Subjects,
Pharmacological Intervention and Design, Task and Analysis Method, and Main Findings. Abbott et al. (2011) involved 79
chronic schizophrenia patients and 114 healthy control subjects, with 7 treated with FGAs; 72 treated with SGAs in a cross-
sectional design, analyzing an auditory-motor task using independent component analysis (beta-weights of regression to task),
revealing task-modulation of task and default mode networks decreased in FGA-treated patients compared to SGA-treated
patients and with higher doses. Lui et al. (2010) studied 34 antipsychotic-naïve, first-episode schizophrenia patients and 34
healthy control subjects, with SGAb treatment in a longitudinal design, scanned before and after 6 weeks of treatment, using
resting-state and seed-voxel correlation and independent component analysis, showing fronto-temporal hyperconnectivity in
patients compared to control subjects at baseline, normalizing after treatment, with no change in default mode network
connectivity which was increased at baseline in patients compared to controls. Sambataro et al. (2010) examined 17
schizophrenia patients (13 medication-naïve and 6 medication-free) and 19 healthy control subjects with olanzapine
monotherapy in a longitudinal design, scanned at 4 and 8 weeks of treatment, using an N-back working memory task and
independent component analysis, finding default mode connectivity increased in the precuneus and inferior parietal lobe, and
decreased in the posterior cingulate cortex in patients compared to controls, with increased connectivity with treatment of the
medial prefrontal cortex to the default mode network in patients. Schlosser et al. (2003) analyzed 12 chronic schizophrenia
patients and 6 healthy control subjects, with 6 SGAb-treated (5 with olanzapine, 1 with quetiapine) and 6 FGAa-treated with
haloperidol in a cross-sectional design, using an N-back working memory task and structural equation modeling of cerebello-
subcortico-cortical effective connectivity, revealing that schizophrenia patients displayed decreased prefronto-cerebellar and
cerebello-thalamus connectivity, and increased thalamo-prefrontal connectivity compared to controls, with dysconnectivity
more pronounced in FGAa-treated compared to SGAb-treated patients, and increased connectivity from left dorsolateral
prefrontal cortex to left parietal cortex and between ventrolateral and dorsolateral prefrontal cortex in patients treated with
FGAa compared to SGAb-treated patients. Lastly, Stephan et al. (2001) focused on 6 patients medication-free (4 drug-naïve; 2
previously FGAa-treated) and 6 healthy control subjects with olanzapine monotherapy in a longitudinal study, scanned before
and after 3 weeks of treatment, using a finger-tapping task and seed-voxel correlation analysis, showing decreased cerebellar
connectivity with mediodorsal thalamus and increased cerebellar connectivity with lateral and medial prefrontal cortex after
treatment, alongside a tendency for connectivity to normalize post-treatment.
